question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
How old is the patient?
Is s/he conscious?
Is s/he breathing?
Is s/he breathing normally or is it difficult/labored?
Can s/he speak in full sentences or only a few words at a time?
Do you hear any wheezing or whistling sound when s/he breathes?
Do you hear a high-pitched sound when s/he breathes in?
Are the lips or fingertips turning blue or gray?
Is s/he using the muscles in the neck or between the ribs to breathe?
When did the breathing difficulty start?
Did it come on suddenly or gradually?
Was s/he eating or drinking when it started? Could something be stuck in the throat?
Does s/he have asthma or COPD?
Does s/he have an inhaler or nebulizer? Has s/he used it?
How many times has s/he used the inhaler today?
Does s/he have any allergies? Has s/he been exposed to any allergens?
Is there any swelling of the face, tongue, or throat?
Does s/he have a fever or cough?
Is s/he coughing up anything? What color is it?
Does s/he have any chest pain?
Has s/he had a recent surgery or been on bed rest?
Does s/he have a history of heart failure?
Is there swelling in the ankles or legs?
Is s/he on home oxygen? What is the flow rate?
Has s/he had episodes like this before? Was s/he ever intubated?
Can s/he cough forcefully?
ECHO-level: Not breathing — respiratory arrest
ECHO-level: Choking — complete obstruction, cannot speak or cough
DELTA-level: Severe respiratory distress with cyanosis
DELTA-level: Stridor with drooling and unable to swallow
DELTA-level: Anaphylaxis — throat swelling with breathing difficulty
DELTA-level: Unable to speak, tripod positioning, accessory muscle use
condition: cannot speak AND tripod AND accessory muscles

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Moderate distress, speaks only a few words at a time
CHARLIE-level: Known asthma, inhaler not helping after multiple doses
CHARLIE-level: Acute dyspnea with history of CHF and lower extremity edema
BRAVO-level: Wheezing with some improvement after inhaler
BRAVO-level: Moderate dyspnea with fever and productive cough
BRAVO-level: Breathing difficulty with recent surgery or immobility
ALPHA-level: Mild shortness of breath, speaking full sentences, no distress signs
condition: mild dyspnea AND speaking full sentences

question_text: ALPHA-level: Known COPD with slight worsening, baseline near-normal
OMEGA-level: Anxiety-related hyperventilation, no other symptoms
Have the patient sit upright. Do NOT let them lie flat.
If the patient has a prescribed inhaler, help them use it now — 2 puffs, wait 1 minute, repeat if needed.
If the patient has an EpiPen and you suspect anaphylaxis, help inject it into the outer thigh.
Loosen any tight clothing around the chest, neck, and waist.
If the patient is choking and conscious, perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver): stand behind, make a fist above the navel, thrust inward and upward.
If the patient becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR immediately.
If on home oxygen, increase the flow rate by 1-2 liters per minute.
Encourage slow, controlled breathing — in through the nose, out through pursed lips.
Stay on the line with me. Help is on the way.
If the breathing gets worse or s/he stops breathing, call us back immediately.
